title: Cry the Divided Country
summary: Amid the controversy this past week surrounding the decision of New Orleans mayor Mitch Landrieu to remove Confederate monuments from the city, especially that of Robert E. Lee, taken down by men in bullet-proof vests and at night, I would like to offer a phrase from a speech I gave at Harvard University some years ago. The occasion was a Festschrift (memorial) for the late Professor Ernest May: "The South's war, brilliantly fought, was not only a lost cause. It was a bad cause."
body: Amid the controversy this past week surrounding the decision of New Orleans mayor Mitch Landrieu to remove Confederate monuments from the city, especially that of Robert E. Lee, taken down by men in bullet-proof vests and at night, I would like to offer a phrase from a speech I gave at Harvard University some years ago. The occasion was a Festschrift (memorial) for the late Professor Ernest May: The South's war, brilliantly fought, was not only a lost cause. It was a bad cause. The South has never fully accepted this. Instead, its reaction has morphed into a kind of super-patriotism. But the stain of slavery remains, as was evident in the repressed racism we saw throughout the presidency of Barack Obama and which was a factor in the reasons why Obama's term was not the unalloyed success that many hoped it would be. * * * Robert E. Lee had been in the Union Army and, together with his subordinate, Jeb Stuart, had put down the putsch of John Brown at the Harpers Ferry armory a few years before the Civil War. Lee made the fateful decision to leave the Army and join Virginia, his native state, when the war broke out. It was at a time when loyalties to the states was still strong. During the Civil War, Lee turned out to be the equal of Ulysses as a Grim Reaper of men, though it was Grant, in the end, who overwhelmed Lee. Grant, despite his bibliousness in the field, was the author of a presidential autobiography that is a gem in American history. _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article reflects on the removal of Confederate monuments in New Orleans, using the Robert E. Lee statue as a focal point. It argues the Confederate cause was not only lost but morally flawed, a reality the South has never fully accepted, instead transforming its reaction into a form of super-patriotism. The author contends the enduring stain of slavery and repressed racism persisted through the Obama presidency, limiting its potential success. The piece provides historical context on Robert E. Lee's decision to join the Confederacy, noting the strength of state loyalties at the time, and contrasts his military role with that of Ulysses S. Grant, who ultimately defeated him. The commentary frames the monument debate within a broader, unresolved national division over history, legacy, and identity.
result.main_content_zh: 本文以新奥尔良市拆除罗伯特·E·李雕像等邦联纪念碑的争议为切入点，展开对美国历史分裂的反思。作者认为，南方邦联的战争虽骁勇，但不仅是失败的事业，更是邪恶的事业，南方从未完全接受这一点，其反应已演变为一种超级爱国主义。奴隶制的污点持续存在，在奥巴马总统任期内被压抑的种族主义显而易见，这也是其任期未能如许多人希望那般完全成功的一个因素。文章提供了罗伯特·E·李在内战爆发时选择效忠家乡弗吉尼亚而非联邦军队的历史背景，指出当时对州的忠诚仍很强烈，并对比了李与最终战胜他的尤利西斯·格兰特。评论将纪念碑之争置于美国关于历史、遗产与身份认同的更深层、未解决的国家分裂框架之中。
